questions.questionText: Let N denote the set of all natural numbers. Define two binary relations on N as R = {(x, y) $$ \in $$ N $$ \times $$ N : 2x + y = 10} and R2 = {(x, y) $$ \in $$ N $$ \times $$ N : x + 2y = 10}. Then :
options: Both R<sub>1</sub> and R<sub>2</sub> are symmetric relations.
Both R<sub>1</sub> and R<sub>2</sub> are transitive relations.
questions.subject: Mathematics

questions.questionText:
Let $\mathrm{X}=\mathbf{R} \times \mathbf{R}$. Define a relation R on X as :

$$\left(a_1, b_1\right) R\left(a_2, b_2\right) \Leftrightarrow b_1=b_2$$

Statement I: $\quad \mathrm{R}$ is an equivalence relation.

Statement II : For some $(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}) \in \mathrm{X}$, the $\operatorname{set} \mathrm{S}=\{(x, y) \in \mathrm{X}:(x, y) \mathrm{R}(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b})\}$ represents a line parallel to $y=x$.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options:
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Statement - I :

Reflexive : $\left(a_1, b_1\right) R\left(a_1, b_1\right) \Rightarrow b_1=b_1 \quad$ True

$\left.\begin{array}{rl}\text { Symmetric : } & \left(\mathrm{a}_1, \mathrm{~b}_1\right) \mathrm{R}\left(\mathrm{a}_2, \mathrm{~b}_2\right) \Rightarrow \mathrm{b}_1=\mathrm{b}_2 \\ & \left(\mathrm{a}_2, \mathrm{~b}_2\right) \mathrm{R}\left(\mathrm{a}_1, \mathrm{~b}_1\right) \Rightarrow \mathrm{b}_2=\mathrm{b}_1\end{array}\right\}$ True

$\left.\begin{array}{ll}\text { Transitive: } & \left(a_1, b_1\right) R\left(a_2, b_2\right) \Rightarrow b_1=b_2 \\ & \&\left(a_2, b_2\right) R\left(a_3, b_3\right) b_2=b_3\end{array}\right\} b_1=b_3$

$$\Rightarrow\left(\mathrm{a}_1, \mathrm{~b}_1\right) \mathrm{R}\left(\mathrm{a}_3 \cdot \mathrm{~b}_3\right) \Rightarrow \text { True }$$

Hence Relation $R$ is an equivence relation Statement-I is true.

For statement - II $\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\mathrm{b}$ so False

questions.questionText:
Let the relation R on the set $\mathrm{M}=\{1,2,3, \ldots, 16\}$ be given by $\mathrm{R}=\{(x, y): 4 y=5 x-3, x, y \in \mathrm{M}\}$.

Then the minimum number of elements required to be added in R , in order to make the relation symmetric, is equal to
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution: $$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & M=\{1,2,3 \ldots .16\} \\ & R=\{(x, y) / 4 y=5 x-3, x, y \in N\} \\ & 4 y=5 x-3 \\ & y=\frac{5 x-3}{4} \end{aligned}\\ &\text { No of order pairs }=4 \end{aligned} $$

questions.questionText: Let A be the set of all functions $f: \mathbf{Z} \rightarrow \mathbf{Z}$ and R be a relation on A such that $\mathrm{R}=\{(\mathrm{f}, \mathrm{g}): f(0)=\mathrm{g}(1)$ and $f(1)=\mathrm{g}(0)\}$. Then R is :
options:
Symmetric and transitive but not reflective
 Symmetric but neither reflective nor transitive
 Transitive but neither reflexive nor symmetric
 Reflexive but neither symmetric nor transitive
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
For $R$ to be reflexive, $(f, f)$ must be in $R$.

The means $f(0)=f(1)$ and $f(1)=f(0)$ must be true for all $f$.

But $f(0) \neq f(1)$ always

Therefore, $R$ is not reflexive

If $(f, g) \in R$, then $f(0)=g(1)$ and $f(1)=g(0)$

$$\begin{aligned} & \because \quad f(0)=g(1) \Rightarrow g(1)=f(0) \\ & \text { and } f(1)=g(0) \Rightarrow g(0)=f(1) \end{aligned}$$

$R$ is symmetric

If $(f, g) \in R$ and $(g, h) \in \mathrm{R}$, then $f(0)=g(1)$, $f(1)=g(0), g(0)=n(1) \& g(1)=h(0)$

Since, $f(0)=g(1)$ and $g(1)=h(0)$, then $f(0)$ is not necessarily equal to $h(0)$.

Therefore, $R$ is not transitive.

$\therefore \quad$ The relation $R$ is symmetric but not reflexive or transitive.

questions.questionText:
Let A and B be two sets containing four and
two elements respectively. Then, the number
of subsets of the set A $\times$ B , each having atleast
three elements are
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Given,

$$ \begin{aligned} &n(A)=4, n(B) =2 \\\\ &\Rightarrow n(A \times B) =8 \end{aligned} $$

Total number of subsets of set $(A \times B)=2^8$

Number of subsets of set $A \times B$ having no element (i.e. $\phi)=1$

Number of subsets of set $A \times B$ having one element $={ }^8 C_1$

Number of subsets of set $A \times B$ having two elements $={ }^8 C_2$

$\therefore$ Number of subsets having atleast three elements

$$ \begin{aligned} &=2^8-\left(1+{ }^8 C_1+{ }^8 C_2\right) \\\\ &=2^8-1-8-28 \\\\ &=2^8-37 \\\\ &=256-37=219 \end{aligned} $$

questions.questionText: In a class of 140 students numbered 1 to 140, all even numbered students opted Mathematics course, those whose number is divisible by 3 opted Physics course and those whose number is divisible by 5 opted Chemistry course. Then the number of students who did not opt for any of the three courses is
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
We're given that there are 140 students numbered from 1 to 140.

1. Define the set $A$ to be the set of even numbered students. The cardinality of $A$ (the number of elements in $A$), denoted as $n(A)$, can be computed as the greatest integer less than or equal to $140/2$. Hence, $n(A) = \left[\frac{140}{2}\right] = 70$. ([.] denotes greatest integer function)

2. Similarly, let $B$ be the set of students whose number is divisible by 3. Hence, $n(B) = \left[\frac{140}{3}\right] = 46$. ([.] denotes greatest integer function)

3. Let $C$ be the set of students whose number is divisible by 5. Hence, $n(C) = \left[\frac{140}{5}\right] = 28$.

So far, we've found the number of students who opted for Mathematics ($n(A)$), Physics ($n(B)$), and Chemistry ($n(C)$).

We also need to consider the students who have opted for multiple subjects :

1. $n(A \cap B)$ represents the count of numbers that are divisible by both 2 and 3 (i.e., divisible by 6).

So, $n(A \cap B) = \left[\frac{140}{6}\right] = 23$.

2. $n(B \cap C)$ represents the count of numbers that are divisible by both 3 and 5 (i.e., divisible by 15).

So, $n(B \cap C) = \left[\frac{140}{15}\right] = 9$.

3. $n(C \cap A)$ represents the count of numbers that are divisible by both 2 and 5 (i.e., divisible by 10).

So, $n(C \cap A) = \left[\frac{140}{10}\right] = 14$.

Finally, $n(A \cap B \cap C)$ represents the count of numbers that are divisible by 2, 3, and 5 (i.e., divisible by 30).

So, $n(A \cap B \cap C) = \left[\frac{140}{30}\right] = 4$.

Now we use the principle of inclusion and exclusion to compute the number of students who have opted for at least one subject. The principle states :

$$n(A \cup B \cup C) = n(A) + n(B) + n(C) - n(A \cap B) - n(B \cap C) - n(C \cap A) + n(A \cap B \cap C)$$

Substituting the values we calculated above :

$$n(A \cup B \cup C) = (70+46+28) - (23+9+14) + 4 = 102$$

Hence, the number of students who opted for at least one subject is 102. Therefore, the number of students who did not opt for any of the subjects is

Total $$-$$ n(A $$ \cup $$ B $$ \cup $$ C)

= 140 $$-$$ 102 = 38

questions.questionText: Let $A = \{2, 3, 5, 7, 9\}$. Let $R$ be the relation on $A$ defined by $xRy$ if and only if $2x \leq 3y$. Let $l$ be the number of elements in $R$, and $m$ be the minimum number of elements required to be added in $R$ to make it a symmetric relation. Then $l + m$ is equal to:
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
For $x=2$

$$ egin{aligned} & 4 \leq 3 y \quad y \in\{2,3,5,7,9\} \ & (2,2)(2,3)(2,5)(2,7)(2,5) \end{aligned} $$

For $x=3$

$$ egin{aligned} & 6 \leq 3 y y \in\{2,3,5,7,9\} \ & (3,2)(3,3)(3,5)(3,7)(3,5) \end{aligned} $$

For $x=5$

$$ 10 \leq 3 y y \in\{5,7,9\} $$

$$ egin{aligned} & (5,5)(5,7)(5,5) \ & ext { For } x=7 \ & 14 \leq 3 y y \in\{5,7,9\} \ & (7,5)(7,7)(7,9) \ & ext { For } x=9 \ & 18 \leq 3 y y \in\{7,9\} \ & (9,7)(9,9) \ & R=5+5+3+3+2=18 \ & (2,3) \in R ext { but }(3,2) \in R(\operatorname{symmetric}) \ & (2,5) \in R ext { but }(5,2) otin R \operatorname{Add}(5,2) \ & (2,7) \in R ext { but }(7,2) otin R \operatorname{Add}(7,2) \ & (2,9) \in R ext { but }(9,2) otin R \operatorname{Add}(9,2) \ & (3,5) \in R ext { but }(5,3) otin R \operatorname{Add}(5,3) \ & (3,7) \in R ext { but }(7,3) otin R \operatorname{Add}(7,3) \ & (3,9) \in R ext { but }(9,3) otin R \operatorname{Add}(9,3) \ & (5,7) \in R ext { but }(7,5) \in R \ & (5,9) \in R ext { but }(9,5) otin R \operatorname{Add}(9,5) \ & (7,9) \in R ext { but }(9,7) \in R \ & 18+7=25 \end{aligned} $$

questions.questionText:
Let $\mathrm{S}=\mathbf{N} \cup\{0\}$. Define a relation R from S to $\mathbf{R}$ by :

$$ \mathrm{R}=\left\{(x, y): \log _{\mathrm{e}} y=x \log _{\mathrm{e}}\left(\frac{2}{5}\right), x \in \mathrm{~S}, y \in \mathbf{R}\right\} . $$

Then, the sum of all the elements in the range of $R$ is equal to :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$$\begin{aligned} & S=\{0,1,2,3 \ldots . .\} \\ & \log _{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{y}=\log _{\mathrm{e}}\left(\frac{2}{5}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\left(\frac{2}{5}\right)^{\mathrm{x}} \end{aligned}$$

Required

$$\text { Sum }=1+\left(\frac{2}{5}\right)^1+\left(\frac{2}{5}\right)^2+\left(\frac{2}{5}\right)^3+\ldots . .-=\frac{1}{1-\frac{2}{5}}=\frac{5}{3}$$

questions.questionText:
Let R1 and R2 be two relation defined as follows :
R1 = {(a, b) $$ \in $$ R2 : a2 + b2 $$ \in $$ Q} and
R2 = {(a, b) $$ \in $$ R2 : a2 + b2 $$ \notin $$ Q},
where Q is the set of all rational numbers. Then :
options:
Neither R<sub>1</sub>
 nor R<sub>2</sub>
 is transitive.

R<sub>2</sub>
 is transitive but R<sub>1</sub>
 is not transitive.

R<sub>1</sub>
 and R<sub>2</sub>
 are both transitive.

R<sub>1</sub>
 is transitive but R<sub>2</sub>
 is not transitive.
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
For R1 :

Let a = 1 + $$\sqrt 2 $$, b = 1 $$-$$ $$\sqrt 2 $$, c = $${8^{{1 \over 4}}}$$

aR1b : a2 + b2 = 6 $$ \in $$ Q

bR1c : b2 + c2 = 3 $$-$$ 2$$\sqrt 2 $$ + 2$$\sqrt 2 $$ = 3 $$ \in $$ Q

aR1c : a2 + c2 = 3 + 2$$\sqrt 2 $$ + 2$$\sqrt 2 $$ $$ otin $$ Q

$$ herefore $$ R1 is not transitive.

For R2 :

Let a = 1 + $$\sqrt 2 $$, b = $$\sqrt 2 $$, c = 1 $$-$$ $$\sqrt 2 $$

aR2b : a2 + b2 = 5 + 2$$\sqrt 2 $$ $$ otin $$ Q

bR2c : b2 + c2 = 5 $$-$$ 2$$\sqrt 2 $$ $$ otin $$ Q

aR2c : a2 + c2 = 3 + 2$$\sqrt 2 $$ + 3 $$-$$ 2$$\sqrt 2 $$ = 6 $$ \in $$ Q

$$ herefore $$ R2 is not transitive.

questions.questionText:
Two newspapers A and B are published in a city.
It is known that 25% of the city populations reads
A and 20% reads B while 8% reads both A and
B. Further, 30% of those who read A but not B
look into advertisements and 40% of those who
read B but not A also look into advertisements,
while 50% of those who read both A and B look
into advertisements. Then the percentage of the
population who look into advertisement is :-
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
The total population to be 100 (for simplicity's sake) and the percentages can be treated as actual numbers of people in this context.

The percentage of people who read newspaper A is given as 25. However, among these, there are people who read both newspapers A and B, given as 8. To find the number of people who read only newspaper A, we subtract the number of people who read both from the total number of people who read A. That is,

n(A only) = 25 – 8 = 17

Similarly, the number of people who read only newspaper B is calculated as :

n(B only) = 20 – 8 = 12

Now, we are given the percentage of each of these groups that look into the advertisements:

  • 30% of those who read A but not B,
  • 40% of those who read B but not A,
  • 50% of those who read both A and B.

To find the total percentage of the population that looks into advertisements, we add up the contributions from each of these groups. We calculate each group's contribution by multiplying the size of the group by the percentage of that group that looks at advertisements :

= $${{30} \over {100}} \times 17$$ (from A only) + $${{40} \over {100}} \times 12 $$(from B only) + $${{50} \over {100}} \times 8 $$(from both A and B)

= 5.1 (from A only) + 4.8 (from B only) + 4 (from both A and B)

Adding these up, we get

= 13.9

This means that 13.9% of the total population looks into the advertisements.

So, the correct answer is :

Option D : 13.9.

questions.questionText: Let $\mathrm{R}=\left\{(x, y) \in \mathbf{N} \times \mathbf{N}: \log _{\mathrm{e}}(x+y) \leq 2\right\}$. Then the minimum number of elements, required to be added in $R$ to make it a transitive relation, is $\_\_\_\_$ .
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
We have

$$ R=\{(x,y)\in \mathbb{N}\times \mathbb{N}:\ln(x+y)\le 2\}. $$

To make the relation transitive, we first find all pairs already in $R$.

Since

$$ \ln(x+y)\le 2 \iff x+y\le e^2. $$

Now,

$$ e^2 \approx 7.389. $$

So, for natural numbers $x,y$, we must have

$$ x+y\le 7. $$

Hence,

$$ R=\{(x,y)\in \mathbb{N}\times \mathbb{N}:x+y\le 7\}. $$

Now list all such pairs:

  • For $x=1$: $(1,1),(1,2),(1,3),(1,4),(1,5),(1,6)$

  • For $x=2$: $(2,1),(2,2),(2,3),(2,4),(2,5)$

  • For $x=3$: $(3,1),(3,2),(3,3),(3,4)$

  • For $x=4$: $(4,1),(4,2),(4,3)$

  • For $x=5$: $(5,1),(5,2)$

  • For $x=6$: $(6,1)$

So these are the elements of $R$.

A relation is transitive if whenever

$$ (a,b)\in R \quad \text{and} \quad (b,c)\in R, $$

then

$$ (a,c)\in R. $$

We need to find all pairs $(a,c)$ that must be present because of transitivity.

Step 1: Find which middle elements are possible

If $(a,b)\in R$, then

$$ a+b\le 7. $$

If $(b,c)\in R$, then

$$ b+c\le 7. $$

For a fixed $b$, this gives

$$ a\le 7-b,\qquad c\le 7-b. $$

So for each $b$, transitivity demands that all pairs

$$ (a,c)\quad \text{with } 1\le a\le 7-b,\; 1\le c\le 7-b $$

must be in the relation.

Taking union over all possible $b\in \mathbb{N}$ with at least one such pair, we get:

  • For $b=1$: required pairs are all

    $$ (a,c),\quad 1\le a\le 6,\; 1\le c\le 6. $$

This is already the largest possible set forced by transitivity.

So, if relation is to be transitive, it must contain every pair

$$ (a,c)\in \{1,2,3,4,5,6\}\times \{1,2,3,4,5,6\}. $$

That means the transitive closure must at least contain all $36$ pairs

$$ (a,c),\quad 1\le a,c\le 6. $$

Step 2: Count how many of these are already in $R$

Among these $36$ pairs, $R$ already contains those with

$$ a+c\le 7. $$

Count them:

  • $a=1$: $c=1$ to $6$ $\Rightarrow 6$

  • $a=2$: $c=1$ to $5$ $\Rightarrow 5$

  • $a=3$: $c=1$ to $4$ $\Rightarrow 4$

  • $a=4$: $c=1$ to $3$ $\Rightarrow 3$

  • $a=5$: $c=1$ to $2$ $\Rightarrow 2$

  • $a=6$: $c=1$ $\Rightarrow 1$

Total:

$$ 6+5+4+3+2+1=21. $$

So the number of additional pairs needed is

$$ 36-21=15. $$

Step 3: Check that this is sufficient

If we add all missing pairs among

$$ \{1,2,3,4,5,6\}\times \{1,2,3,4,5,6\}, $$

then the relation becomes

$$ \{(a,c):1\le a\le 6,\;1\le c\le 6\}. $$

This is clearly transitive, because whenever

$$ (a,b)\in R \quad \text{and} \quad (b,c)\in R, $$

all three numbers lie in $\{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$, so $(a,c)$ is also in the relation.

Therefore, the minimum number of elements to be added is

$$ \boxed{15} $$

questions.questionText:
Let  A = {n $$ \in $$ N: n is a 3-digit number}

       B = {9k + 2: k $$ \in $$ N}

and C = {9k + $$l$$: k $$ \in $$ N} for some $$l ( 0 < l < 9)$$

If the sum of all the elements of the set A $$ \cap $$ (B $$ \cup $$ C) is 274 $$ \times $$ 400, then $$l$$ is equal to ________.
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
In this problem, we're dealing with 3-digit numbers in set $A$, and subsets $B$ and $C$ which represent numbers of specific forms.

1. First, we consider the numbers of the form $9k + 2$ (Set $B$) within the 3-digit range, which starts at 101 and ends at 992.

2. We calculate the sum of these numbers, denoted as $s_1$. To calculate $s_1$, you use the formula for the sum of an arithmetic series :

$$(n/2) \times (\text{{first term}} + \text{{last term}})$$

Here, $n$ is the total count of such numbers. These are 3-digit numbers of the form $9k + 2$, and we can find the total count by subtracting the smallest such number (101) from the largest (992), dividing the result by 9 (because we're considering numbers with a difference of 9), and then adding 1.

The sum $s_1$ is calculated as follows :

$$(100/2) \times (101 + 992) = 54650$$

3. According to the problem, the sum of all elements of the set $A \cap (B \cup C)$ is $274 \times 400 = 109600$.

Since the set $A \cap (B \cup C)$ is the union of two disjoint sets (the set of all three-digit numbers of form $9k + 2$ and the set of all three-digit numbers of form $9k + l$), we can write this sum as :

$$s_1 $$(for numbers of the form 9k + 2) + $$s_2$$ (for numbers of the form 9k + l) = 109600

4. Solving this equation for $s_2$ (the sum of numbers of the form $9k + l$), we get :

$$s_2 = 109600 - s_1 = 109600 - 54650 = 54950$$

5. The sum $s_2$ can be expressed as $(n/2) \times (\text{{first term}} + \text{{last term}})$, where $n$ is the count of numbers of the form $9k + l$. The first term here is the smallest 3-digit number of this form, which is $99 + l$, and the last term is the largest such number, which is $990 + l$.

We equate this to $s_2$ to solve for $l$ :

$$54950 = (100/2)[(99 + l) + (990 + l)]$$

6. Simplifying this equation, we get :

$$2l + 1089 = 1099$$

Solving for $l$, we find :

$$l = 5$$

So, the correct answer is 5.

questions.questionText: The number of elements in the set $$\{ n \in Z:|{n^2} - 10n + 19| < 6\} $$ is _________.
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Given, $\left|n^2-10 n+19\right|<6$

$\Rightarrow-6 < n^2-10 n+19 < 6$

Take, $-6 < n^2-10 n+19$ and $n^2-10 n+19 < 6$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow n^2-10 n+25 > 0 & \text { and }\quad n^2-10 n+13 < 0 \\\\ \Rightarrow(n-5)^2 > 0 & \text { and } n=\frac{10 \pm \sqrt{100-52}}{2}<0 \end{array} $$

$\Rightarrow n \in \mathbb{Z}-\{5\}$

$$ \begin{array}{lr} & \therefore n \in[5-2 \sqrt{3}, 5+2 \sqrt{3}] \\\\ & \therefore n \in[13,8.3] \\\\ & \therefore n=2,3,4,5,6,7,8 \end{array} $$

Thus, number of element in the set is ' 6 '

questions.questionText:
Let X = {n $$ \in $$ N : 1 $$ \le $$ n $$ \le $$ 50}. If
A = {n $$ \in $$ X: n is a multiple of 2} and
B = {n $$ \in $$ X: n is a multiple of 7}, then the number of elements in the smallest subset of X containing both A and B is ________.
questions.subject: Mathematics

questions.questionText: The sum of all the elements of the set $$\{ \alpha \in \{ 1,2,.....,100\} :HCF(\alpha ,24) = 1\} $$ is __________.
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
The numbers upto 24 which gives g.c.d. with 24 equals to 1 are 1, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19 and 23.

Sum of these numbers = 96

There are four such blocks and a number 97 is there upto 100.

$$\therefore$$ Complete sum

= 96 + (24 $$\times$$ 8 + 96) + (48 $$\times$$ 8 + 96) + (72 $$\times$$ 8 + 96) + 97

= 1633
